generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the ink still drying on the page, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lonely keep. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with a fury I have not heard in many a year. I fear the night will not be kind to me, and I have no doubt that the waves will claim me before dawn. But I write, not for my own sake, but for yours.

Five years have passed since the last letter I sent you, and I daresay five more have passed since the last time we spoke. I do not know if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the memories of our last parting have been buried beneath the weight of time. I would not wish to burden you with the pain of my absence, but I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you, nor have I ceased to think of you.

The world outside this tower has changed, as it always does. The world within has not. I still tend to the beacon each night, though the light now is but a shadow of what it once was. The lantern has grown dim, as have my hands, and the sea has grown restless. The storm that now approaches is not merely a tempest of wind and water—it is a reckoning, a test of will and spirit. I have lived long enough to know that such nights are not for the faint of heart

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast from the deep, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just the lighthouse.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years since you left this house, that once was our home. I still remember the day you walked out, your face set with a fury I could not understand, your eyes filled with a pain I could not bear to see. You said you were leaving to find yourself, but I feared you were leaving to forget me. And I, in my foolishness, let you go. I did not know how to reach you, nor did I know how to stop you.

You were my light, Eliza. The lighthouse is my life, but it is nothing without you. I have spent these years watching the sea, hoping for a sign, a message, a glimpse of you. And yet, the only thing I see is the dark, the storm, and the silence.

Tonight, the sky is black as the abyss, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very stones. I fear for my life, but more than that, I fear for what this storm may take from me. If I do not survive, I hope you will find this letter. I hope you will know that I have not forgotten

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that now rages beyond these walls. The wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea, that old adversary, roars in defiance. I fear I may not see dawn. But if I do, I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written a single word to you since the day you left.

I do not know if you will read this. Perhaps the sea will claim me before the ink dries. But I must say these things, for I have carried them in silence too long.

You were a child when you ran from me, Eleanor. A child with a mind too sharp, a heart too tender, and a soul too restless for the quiet life I offered. I tried to give you a home, a purpose, a sense of stability. But you were not content with the tides or the stars. You wanted more—more than the lighthouse, more than the sea, more than the life I had known. And so you left. You left without a word, without a glance, without even a farewell. You took your dreams and your sorrow with you, and left me with only the echoes of your footsteps.

I have not forgotten. I have not forgiven. But I have not forgotten the way your eyes looked when you stood at the door, your hands clenched at your sides, your voice

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and five years of unspoken words, unshed tears, and the weight of a father’s regret. I have not spoken to you, nor have I dared to hope that you might one day write back. But tonight—tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I feel the need to reach out, to speak, to be heard, even if it is only in the solitude of this lighthouse.

You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter echoing through these stone walls, and the sea would seem to calm just for the sake of your presence. You were my pride, my joy, my everything. But time, like the tides, has carried us apart. I do not know if you remember, but I have always believed that love endures, even when the world tries to tear it down.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I have learned that the sea is both a friend and a foe. It gives us light, but it also demands our loyalty. Tonight, the winds howl with a fury I have not heard in many a year, and the waves crash against the cliffs like a thousand voices crying out in sorrow. I do not know if I will survive this night, and

**April 12th, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, Cape Hesperus**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it too has been waiting for this moment. I have kept the light burning all night, not out of duty alone, but because I know you are out there somewhere—somewhere I cannot reach, and perhaps never will again.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this place, this life, this *family*. I have not heard a word from you since the day you stormed out with that foolish young man, Thomas. I suppose you think I do not know what you did. That I do not know how he led you astray, how he broke your spirit, how he made you believe that the world outside these cliffs was worth more than the quiet, steadfast life we had built here.

But I do know, Eleanor. And I have never stopped wondering if I failed you.

You were always the clever one, the one who saw beyond the horizon, beyond the narrow world of this lighthouse. I was content with the light, the routine, the quiet. You wanted more. And I let you go. I should have held you back, I should have fought for you, but I was too proud, too afraid of what you might say if

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heavy heart, my hands trembling not from the chill of the wind but from the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and though I have tried to keep your memory alive in the quiet moments of this lighthouse, the silence has grown louder with each passing day.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one that has already been whispered about in the village, a tempest that promises to test the very foundations of this old stone tower. I have seen such storms before, and though I have always prided myself on my steadfastness, I must confess, I am not as certain of my strength as I once was. The sea has been restless, and the winds have grown fierce, and I fear that this night may be my last.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor, the one who reminded me that there is more to this world than the endless vigil of the lighthouse. I remember the nights we spent by the fire, your laughter echoing through the halls, and the way your eyes would light up as you spoke of your dreams. You were a beacon, just like the light I keep burning for the ships that pass through the night. I have often wondered if I have been a poor keeper of that light, or if I have simply been too afraid to let it shine through my own heart.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and I fear that

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the cold wind howling like a banshee outside, and the sea roiling as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which this lighthouse stands. The lantern above me flickers, and I feel as though I am standing on the edge of the world. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself longing for your voice more than ever.

I do not know if I will survive this night, Eliza. The sea has been restless for days, and the wind has grown cruel. The old lighthouse is not what it once was, and I fear that even its strength may not hold against what is to come. I have spent my life watching the horizon, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight, I find myself lost in the silence of my own heart.

I have not written to you in years, and I know that words may not be enough to mend what has been broken. But I must try. You were once my light, my guiding star, and now I find myself adrift in a sea of regret. I have been a poor father, a stubborn man, and I have let pride and silence drive the wedge between us. I have not seen you in years, and I have not spoken your name in the way I once did. I have not held you, nor heard your laughter, nor felt your presence in this house

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cliffs. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky, a swirling tempest of black and silver, threatens to swallow the stars whole. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as this storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You were once my pride and joy, my little light in the darkness. I remember the day you came to me with your eyes full of dreams and your heart full of hope. You spoke of the world beyond these rocks, of distant lands and grand adventures. I feared you would leave me behind, and so I held you close, not wanting to let go. But in my fear, I became a stone, unmoving and unyielding, and you, my dear, grew distant.

I have not seen you since the day you left. I know not where you are, nor do I care to know. You were never meant for this life of solitude, of duty, of endless nights watching the sea. You were meant for something greater. And yet, I remain here, a relic of a bygone age, bound to this lighthouse, this lonely tower, this endless vigil.

The storm is coming. I have seen the signs in the sky, the way the waves have grown in fury, the way the wind has

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the cold or the wind, but from the weight of the silence that has grown between us. Five long years have passed since you left, and yet it feels as though the night you walked out of this house was but yesterday. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and I fear I may never find the strength to do so again.

The storm is upon us now, a beast of wind and fury that howls across the sea like a mournful dirge. The lighthouse stands tall, but I do not know if it will stand long enough to see the dawn. The waves have grown monstrous, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost divine. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, but I wonder if it is in vain. If the world has moved on, if the world no longer needs a lighthouse, or a father.

I have always believed that the sea is a cruel and fickle thing, but I have come to think that it is not the sea that is cruel—it is the silence between us. You left, Eleanor, and you left without a word. I have tried to understand, to forgive, to forget. But I cannot. I cannot forget the way your eyes darkened when you saw me, nor the way you turned your back and walked away, as though I were a ghost or a stranger.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from cold, but from the weight of years. Five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years since you left. I’ve tried to forget, but the sea never forgets. It remembers every soul it has ever claimed, and I fear this storm may be the one that takes me.

You were a fire in the night, Eliza. A flame that burned too bright, too fierce, for the world I knew. You left with your dreams and your heart, and I was left with the lighthouse, the salt, and the silence.

I’ve been here through many storms, my girl. I’ve weathered gales that howled like wolves and waves that rose like mountains. But this one… this one feels different. The wind is not just howling—it is whispering. The sea is restless, and the sky is black as ink. The lantern is dim, and I fear the light may not be enough.

I’ve been thinking of you often, more than I care to admit. I’ve watched the sunrise with the same hope you once had for your future, and I’ve cried into the night when the moon was too far to reach. I’ve told myself that you would understand, that you would return, but you never did. And I’ve learned to live with the silence.

I know you must be angry. I know you must think me a fool,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these stone walls, and the sea roiling with a fury I’ve never known. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for you, my dear Eleanor, and for the love that once bound us, though it has since withered like the old lighthouse beam in the rain.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us has grown louder than the waves. I’ve tried to forget the day you walked out, your back turned to me, your voice sharp with words I still cannot recall. You said I was a fool, a man who clung to duty like a sailor to a mast. But I was not wrong to stand by this lighthouse, not when the sea threatened to swallow the village whole.

You were young then, full of fire and fury, and I, a man worn by years and the weight of responsibility. You thought I was too old for love, too rigid for understanding. But I was not your father, Eleanor—I was your keeper, your guardian, your shield against the wrath of the sea. And you, you chose to leave, not for love, but for something else—something I could not fathom.

I have missed you, child. Not the girl you were, but the daughter you might have been. I’ve seen your face in

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that looms on the horizon, for I fear the winds may claim me before I can see the dawn. Yet even in this hour of uncertainty, I find myself compelled to reach out, not with the words I once thought I might, but with the truth that has long been buried beneath years of silence and regret.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the silence has grown colder with each passing day, I have not ceased to think of you. I have seen your name in the ledger of the lighthouse, written in the margins by the young assistant who often comes to relieve me. He speaks of you with a reverence I do not understand, and I have no doubt that you are a woman of strength, of spirit, and of quiet grace. I wish I had known you better, Eleanor. I wish I had not let pride and fear keep me from you.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper’s duty is to guide those who sail the sea, to light the way for others, even as the darkness threatens to swallow them whole. But I have never considered that I, too, might be lost in the darkness of my own making. I have allowed my anger and my shame to cloud my judgment, and in doing so, I have lost the most precious thing of all: the chance to mend what was broken between us.

You were once my greatest pride

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it is more likely to find you in the throes of your own storm—be it of heart or soul. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and with each passing day, the silence between us has grown heavier than the sea itself. I write to you now not out of duty, nor out of the hope of reconciliation, but because I fear the night ahead may be my last.

The wind howls like the devil himself, and the sea, that old, unyielding beast, roars with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, but I feel it tremble in its bones. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray it will guide the ships through the chaos. Yet I know, in my heart, that the light may not be enough.

I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if you remember the face of your father. I was once a man of purpose, of steady hands and a voice that could calm the most restless sea. But time, like the tide, has worn me down. I have grown old, my joints stiff, my breath labored. I have seen the world change, and I have watched my own world shrink to this lonely tower, where the only company is the wind and the waves.

I have no regrets, not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, as the sky darkens and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The sea, once so calm and predictable, roars with fury, and I fear that tonight may be the last time I ever see the sun rise. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if my words will reach you or if you will even read them. But I must try.

You were once my pride and joy, my little star. I remember the day you were born, the way the light from the lighthouse danced on your face as if the sea itself had blessed you. I used to tell you stories of the sea, of the stars, of the great ships that passed by, and of the dangers they faced. You would listen, wide-eyed, and dream of being a sailor one day. But time, as it does, has moved on, and you chose your own path.

I do not know where you are now, nor do I know if you still remember the lighthouse, or if the sea still calls to you. I have not heard from you in years, and I have come to believe that perhaps we have grown apart, like the tides that once met with certainty but now drift in opposite directions. I have tried to write, but the letters always returned unopened. I suppose you no longer want to hear from me.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls of this wretched tower. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is black as a funeral shroud. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but if I do, I must speak these words to you, for they have weighed upon my soul these past five years like the burden of a thousand unspoken truths.

You were twelve when you left, Eliza. A child, yet you took the key to our home and walked away without a word. I tried to follow, but you were gone before I could reach the gate. I have not seen you since, and I have not spoken your name aloud, for fear it would summon you back—like a ghost, or a memory too painful to bear.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have. The light burns, as it must, for those who sail these waters. But I have not kept the fire of my heart. It has long since gone out, smothered by the silence between us. You were my only child, my light in the darkness, and now I am left with only the storm and the sea.

I have heard the tales of your life since you left—of your marriage, your children, the way you have moved on as if I had never been your father. I do not blame you for that. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of a thousand lost souls, and the sea, that old, unrelenting beast, roars in fury against the cliffs. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before the dawn. Yet, even in this hour of darkness, I must write to you—because I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I cannot bear the silence any longer.

You were once my light, my guiding star, the only thing that kept me from drowning in the solitude of this lonely post. But time, like the tide, has worn away the shores of our bond. You left, and I did not chase you. I told myself I was protecting you, but I fear I only hurt you more. You were young, full of fire and dreams, and I, old and stubborn, clung to the past like a man clinging to a driftwood log in a storm. You called me cruel, and I suppose I was. But I was not cruel for cruelty's sake—I was cruel because I did not know how to love you without fear.

I have watched the sea for decades, and I have learned to read its moods. Tonight, it is angry. I have seen it devour ships, swallow men whole, and leave only silence in its wake. I have no illusions about my own fate. I am old, and this l

**The Lighthouse, Cliffs of Dunmore**  
**October 12th, 1893**  

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from age, but from the weight of words I have long kept buried. Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. I have not spoken your name in all that time, nor have I dared to write it. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear I may not survive it. And so, I must say what I have not said before.

You were always a spirited child, Eliza. Brighter than the stars that light our way, more stubborn than the wind that howls through these cliffs. You were meant for more than this lighthouse, this endless watching of the sea, this life of salt and solitude. You were meant for the world beyond, for the grand halls of London, for the salons and the society. You were not meant to be left behind, as I was.

I remember the night you left. It was a storm, like tonight, though not as fierce. You stood at the door, your coat wrapped tight against the wind, your eyes burning with a fire I could not understand. "You will never understand," you said, "why I had to go." And I did not. I did not understand

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Time has a cruel way of slipping through one's fingers, and I have not written to you in five long, weary years. The world has moved on, and I, it seems, have been left behind, clinging to the flickering light of the lighthouse like a moth to a flame. 

Tonight, the sea is restless. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have spent my life guarding this beacon, ensuring that no soul is lost to the dark, yet tonight, I feel the weight of the storm pressing upon me with a force I cannot quite comprehend. I am not certain I shall survive it, and I fear that if I do not, my final words may never reach you. 

You were once my greatest joy, and I have often found solace in the memory of your laughter, the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of your dreams. I have missed you more than I can say, and I know that my silence has been a wound to you as well. I have been a fool, Eleanor, to let pride and fear drive the distance between us. I should have reached out, should have held you close when the world felt too heavy to bear.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**  

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, not from fear of the storm that now roils the sea, but from the weight of the silence between us. Five years have passed since the last letter you sent, and though I've kept the lamp burning, I've kept the door to my heart locked as well.  

The wind howls like a beast at the gate, and the sea, that ever-hungry thing, roars with a voice that sounds like your name. I've seen it before—this storm. I’ve weathered many, but this one feels different. The sky is black, the waves come in with a fury that seems to know the hour, and I fear the light may not hold.  

I’ve not spoken of you in years, but tonight, as the lantern’s glow flickers against the dark, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a lifetime. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes grew cold as you said, “You don’t understand,” and I believed you. I believed I had failed you.  

But I’ve been thinking… what if I did understand? What if I had only ever wanted to keep you safe, even if it meant keeping you at a distance? The sea is cruel, and the world is full of things that can break a heart. I thought I could protect you from them. I thought I could

**My Dearest Elara,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I daresay I write with a heavy heart. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world has turned many times upon its axis, I have not forgotten the day you left this house, with your head held high and your eyes full of fire. I have not forgotten your words—*“You are not the man I once knew, Father.”* I have carried them like a stone upon my chest, heavy and unyielding.

I write to you now not from the comfort of this old lighthouse, but from the edge of despair. Tonight, a storm is coming—one of the worst I have ever seen. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that great and terrible beast, roars with fury. I have spent the day preparing the lantern, ensuring the oil is fresh, the wick is trimmed, and the light is ready to guide the lost and the damned. But I fear, my dear Elara, that even the light may not be enough to save me.

You once said that I was a man of duty, a man who placed the needs of others above my own. You were right. I have always been a man of duty. But I have also been a man of silence, a man who let the world pass him by without a word. I have not written to you, nor have

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee beyond the stone walls of this old lighthouse, and the sea, that ever-hungry beast, roiling in its wrath. The lamps have long since been extinguished, and I have lit only a single candle, its flickering light barely enough to see by. Yet I write, for I know you are not here, and I fear I may never see you again.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have done little to soften the ache of your absence. I have watched the tides come and go, the stars shift in their endless dance, and the seasons turn like the hands of a broken clock. But I have not watched for you, not truly. Not since the day you stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with a sorrow I could not bear to understand.

You called me a monster, Eleanor. A man who could not love, who could not hold you close. And perhaps I was. I was a man who had given you the world, and yet you left it behind. I was a man who had tried to be a father, and yet I failed you in the only way that matters. I was a man who had watched you grow into the woman you are, and I could not bear the thought of losing you.

But I do not write this letter to beg for your forgiveness, nor to ask

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my back, the sea churning with a fury that makes my bones tremble. The lighthouse has stood for over a century, and yet tonight feels like the end of something far older—something I once believed was unbreakable.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of hollow mornings and endless nights, of watching the beacon burn with a light that never reaches you. I’ve kept the lamp lit, not just for the sailors, but for the hope that one day, you might return. But the sea has been cruel, and so has time.

I have not spoken your name in so long, and yet it lingers on my lips like a prayer. I’ve tried to forget the last time we saw each other, the way your eyes hardened when you said, *“You don’t understand.”* I thought I did. I thought I could protect you from the world, from the truths I kept buried in the sand. But I was wrong. I was never the father you needed. I was just a man too afraid to show you the cracks in my own heart.

Tonight, the storm is coming. It has already begun to claw at the cliffs, the waves crashing against the stone like a thousand mourners. I’ve spent my life watching the sea, but tonight it feels like it is watching me. I’ve been preparing for this for

**April 3rd, 1889**  
**Lighthouse, Point Eleanor**  
**County of Devon, England**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast at my door. The sea, that ever-watchful thing, roars with fury, and the sky is a canvas of black and white, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I do not know if I shall see morning, but if I do, I must write this. I must make you understand.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of your letters that I kept, folded and sealed, like letters from a ghost. I have not forgotten you, Eliza. I have not forgotten the day you left, the day you chose your own path, and I chose to keep the light burning, as I always have.

You were a child when you left, and I thought it was for the best. You were restless, full of questions, full of dreams. I could not bear to hold you back. I thought I was protecting you. But now, as the storm approaches and the wind threatens to tear the roof from this tower, I wonder if I was wrong.

I have always believed in the light. It is my duty, my purpose, my life. But tonight, I feel the weight of the sea pressing against my chest, and I wonder if

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now not with the ease of a man who has the luxury of time, but with the weight of a soul burdened by years of silence and regret. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though the days have dragged on, they have not dulled the ache of your absence. I have not spoken to you, nor have I tried. Perhaps it was easier that way. But tonight… tonight is different.

The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind has grown more fierce with each passing hour. The lighthouse, my life’s work, stands as it always has—steadfast, unyielding. But even the sturdiest stone can crack under the weight of the storm. I fear for my life, Eleanor. I fear for the light that guides the ships, and I fear for the silence that has grown between us.

I have not forgotten the last time we spoke. The words were sharp, the air thick with unspoken grievances. I do not claim to have been right in those days. **My Dearest Eliza,**

It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and though the sea has seen many a storm, I have never known such a tempest as the one that has taken hold of my heart. I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the solemnity of one who stands on the edge of the abyss, knowing that words may not reach you in time.

The wind howls like the devil himself, and the waves, in their fury, seem to rise like a living thing, clawing at the very foundation of this lighthouse. I have seen the light flicker, and I fear it may be the last time it will shine upon the horizon. Tonight, my dear, may be the last night I shall see the stars.

I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the sorrow that followed. I have not forgotten the silence that settled upon our home, nor the way your mother’s eyes grew heavy with grief when you refused to look at me.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet it feels like only yesterday that you stood at the door of our home, your eyes filled with a sorrow I could not then understand, nor now fully comprehend.

The wind howls outside like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me. I fear the storm may take more than just the light from our tower—it may take me, and with me, the last thread of the life we once shared.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones upon which I stand. I have not seen you in five years, and I fear I may not live to see you again. Yet I must write, for I cannot bear the thought of leaving this world without a final word.

And I let you go, as I always have—because I believed it was for your own good. I believed I was protecting you from the darkness that I, too, had once known.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**To My Dearest Elara,**

**April 12, 1887**  
**Lighthouse, Point Marlowe**  

My Dearest Clara,

**April 12th, 1897**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold wind that howls through the cliffs but from the weight of silence that has hung between us for five long years. You were once my greatest pride, my light in the darkness of this lonely life upon the rocks. Now, I fear that light has long since faded from my world.

It has been a cruel winter, my dear, and the sea has been restless, as though it knows the storm is coming. The lantern has been lit every night, as it has always been, but tonight—tonight, the sky turns black, and the wind screams like a beast in pain. I have seen the clouds gather in the east, thick and heavy, and I know that this is no ordinary squall. It is a tempest, a thing of nature’s wrath, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I have not sent a letter, nor have I called upon you. I have let the silence grow, and now I wonder if it is too late to mend what has been broken. You were once my heart, my joy, my reason for keeping this lantern burning in the night. Now, I wonder if I have only kept it for myself, for the comfort of a familiar light in an ever-darkening world.

I do not know
